tax: Gust Whirler is placed at species rank beneath Whirler (Aeolian order, Air Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a contained spinning air-column, debris flung from ledges, and stilling when dispersed. It is separated from its sibling the Pall Choke-Wind by its fast spinning gust versus the Choke-Wind's slow settling foul air; the two share the genus body-plan but never the foul still air. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: bounded shaft-air held in coherent spin by air-pressure.
ap: Gust Whirler stands 2–4 m tall spinning column. Its silhouette is a whirling column visible only through caught dust and grit. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: caught dust and grit spiralling in a column where the air should be still; a rising whistle and tug felt before it is seen; and loose debris lifted and circling at its base. It moves with a swift drifting spin along shafts and draught-lines, fastest in vertical shafts, and shows none; invisible but for its debris. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a clean-blown patch ringed by flung debris and a scoured ledge, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Gust Whirler persists in vertical shafts, draught-swept galleries, and chimney passages where moving air collects and channels. It does not breed; it forms where air-pressure animates a shaft draught into a coherent spinning column, and recurs whenever the draught resumes and the spin re-coheres. It sustains its mass on air-pressure and channelled draught, drawing in moving air drawn into its spin. Range is bounded by the draught-fed shaft: outside that band the body stills into ordinary air where the draught fails. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a shaft where dust spirals upward against the draught with no wind to explain it, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Gust Whirler is roused by a crew on a ledge or rope, or a change in the draught and otherwise lies a still draught indistinguishable from ordinary shaft air. It claims a single shaft or chimney and answers intrusion with spinning up to fling crews from ledges and ropes. Toward grey-folk it is a fall hazard on exposed ledges; survey crews report that Wall-Wardens clip double on any ledge where dust spirals the wrong way. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Gust Whirler: a body of bounded shaft-air held in coherent spin by air-pressure; immune to piercing and cutting, which pass through it; and a defining weakness — enclosure and dead-air pockets, which collapse its spin. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-pressure and motion sensing. It tolerates darkness, heat, and cold short of freezing but fails at enclosure and loss of draught. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the spinning column and lifted-debris ring, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Gust Whirler are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it flings crews and debris from ledges and ropes; it lifts and hurls loose stone and gear; it draws in draught to rebuild its spin; and it stills suddenly to drop a hauled victim. Each is a direct expression of bounded air held in coherent spin, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, hold form in dead air, or strike with mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Gust Whirler matters because it flings crews from exposed ledges, and Wall-Wardens now double-clip where dust spirals wrongly. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a fall hazard logged with shafts and exposed ledges, and its presence reshapes routes through the vertical shafts, now rigged with double-clip lines where draughts spin. Dust spiralling upward against the draught is the Wall-Wardens' sign a Whirler has formed.
g: Gust Whirler (Ventus vertens) is an air elemental that spins as a contained whirlwind in a shaft and flings loose debris and crews from ledges. Documented from the vertical shafts and draught-swept galleries of the tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a coherent spinning column of air made visible only by the dust it carries — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Its body is bounded air given spin and will, invisible but for the debris it lifts. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden stilling of the air and a rain of dropped debris and no carcass.
